zotero.abstractNote: In many psychophysical experiments, observers are asked to judge the similarity of stimuli. A common way to analyze such data is to embed the stimuli into a low-dimensional Euclidean space, a technique known as multidimensional scaling (MDS). A crucial, yet often overlooked, question is how many dimensions are actually needed to represent the perceived similarity structure. If the chosen dimension is too low, the embedding will be inaccurate; if it is too high, the model will overfit. In this paper, we propose a novel method to estimate the intrinsic dimension of the perceived stimulus space. Our approach is based on triplet comparisons, which are a robust and common way to collect similarity data. We use the accuracy of triplet predictions as a measure of model fit and develop a hypothesis test to determine the minimum number of dimensions required to explain the data. We demonstrate the effectiveness of our method on both synthetic and real-world psychophysical data, showing that it provides a reliable estimate of the perceived dimension and helps to avoid overfitting.
